result:
The Liquid Hour

I woke at 3:17 a.m. again. Not with a gasp or a scream—nothing theatrical—but with the sense that the darkness in my room had developed a temperature, something denser than night should ever be. It pressed just above my ribcage like the heel of a practiced hand, coaxing, not crushing. A gentle invitation. My watch, glowing tiny cobalts, confirmed what my heartbeat already knew: 3:17. It has been 3:17 for weeks, or maybe sixteen years; the distinction blurs across the pre-dawn, the brain too magnesium-poor to draw a straight line.

Outside, the city exhales a low metallic hum, the kind of noise you only hear when you’ve slept in bursts, like a radio antenna catching stations that were never meant for daylight. I sit up in bed and wait, not for any particular bodily need—my bladder is quiet, my mouth only ordinarily dry—but for whatever seeps in at the edges of 3:17. I have begun to think of this hour as liquid, thinned by insomnia and poured between yesterday and tomorrow. Tonight it pools, iridescent as an oil slick. I dip a fingertip in.

When I first moved to this seventh-floor apartment, there was a falcon that roosted on the fire escape. It arrived only at night, a peregrine, slate-gray helmet and eyes narrowed to onyx commas. I named it Magnetic North, because every time it turned its head, the rest of the visible sky adjusted slightly, like iron filings aligning. We developed a ritual: I cracked the window at 3:00 a.m.; it arrived seventeen minutes later, without fail. We watched each other until the first violet welts of dawn, and then it was gone, skinning away like a thrown knife. Somewhere in the cold cord of its wings there was an explanation for why I kept waking, but explanations are coins that always land on edge when thrown between species.

When the migration reversed and the falcon didn’t come back, I began hearing something else: a woman’s voice, soft, rising from the elevator shaft that winds behind the bedroom wall. She recited numbers—safe primes and lucky primes and primes that are the difference of two cubes—slow as breath, deliberate as a funeral mass. I sat on the parquet floor, ear pressed to cold plaster, and let the recitation lull me into half-dreams where mathematics was a tint behind my eyelids. The voice never once miscounted. I checked.

Tonight, however, the voice is different. Not recital but conversation, as if she has finally found the person she was waiting for. I can’t pick out words, just the contours of chat: question, hesitant answer, laughter like beads spilling on marble. I’ve never heard her laugh. I press my palm flat against the wall and feel, unmistakably, an answering pulse: human warm. A small pushback, as if her hand is also there, consoling or begging or saying yes, finally. An ache blooms in my sternum wide enough to pass a fist through.

If I were a braver person I would wrench the paneling free and crawl down the stuttering florescent guts of the building until I found her. But bravery, I’ve learned, is not my genre. I specialize in the small, sturdy magic of hesitation. My apartment is littered with optimistic but unfinished spells: the basil I sipped in whisper-chants until it withered brown, the mason jar still half-filled with coins that were supposed to “attract.” I am good at beginnings; middles and ends, like closing parenthesis, elude.

Eventually I stand, an automatic unfolding. My bare feet shape cold footprints across the floorboard’s memory. I open the fridge just for the blunt light. Inside: half a grapefruit aging marmalade-like within its pith, a bottle of Hungarian pear eau-de-vie, and a sealed dish labelled “do not open until (empty).” My handwriting, clearly, though I don’t remember writing it. The dish rattles faintly, like a moth tapping glass. I leave it undisturbed.

I flick the overhead kitchen light. The bulb sighs, then burns an anaemic orange, enough to see my reflection smashed across six mirrored tiles above the stove. Six selves converge, slightly out of step. One self has wet eyes. Another scratches her collarbone. The sixth—a delayed playback—leans forward and mouths, Hurry. I do not obey.

Instead I pour two centimetres of pear brandy into a juice glass. The scent transports me ten summers back, to my grandmother’s orchard at dusk, when the fruit hung so heavy the boughs looked humpbacked. She set white sheets beneath each tree and taught me to shake the branches just so: a pear rainfall, soft and lethal. “Catch them gently,” she warned. “They’ve had the sky in their mouths all day; they bruise if you remind them of coming down.” In the mornings the lawn was silvered with fallen wasps and dew-pearls; I walked barefoot, careful, eating only the survivors. I drink the brandy and the kitchen tilts; the tiles slide their mirrors shut.

3:24. There are rules to liquid hours: at 3:24 you may ask one Question. Not out loud—sound ruptures—but by shaping it with every cell in your body until the Question becomes a posture. My Question, arrived fully formed tonight: If every small irreversible thing I’ve unsaid still swims somewhere in me, can it be lured back before the architecture of my life calcifies?

I sit cross-legged on the living room rug—navy, patterned with cream rhomboids, a gift from an ex who moved to Auckland and converted to Buddhism. Under my thighs the fibers hum like old phone wires. I breathe into the asking. My lungs balloon, empty, balloon again. The room pulses gently. Somewhere between inhale and exhale the rug loosens its geometry; the rhomboids float free and orbit around my knees like cautious satellites. I hold still. One shape presses a cool point against my ankle, another taps my cheekbone with a mother whisper, Shhh, little planet, everything orbits exactly where it must.

At 3:31 the apartment door rattles. Just once. Not a knock—more like the hallway itself clearing its throat. I consider ignoring it. My Question glimmers hopeful inside its rib-basket; I almost want to cradle it to completion. But doors are hinges between worlds, and this one bears the metallic smell of rain, even though no weather report predicted any.

I stand, knees pop. The mirrored tiles have already dimmed, leaving vague after-images that I try not to interpret. My hand finds the cold knob; I turn. In the hallway stands a child, maybe seven, barefoot, in a stained yellow T-shirt advertising a 1988 Smashing Pumpkins tour that never existed yet. Her hair is chopped blunt at the jaw. She holds my lost copy of The Secret Garden, the one with pages fatted by saltwater. The spine is cracked open to Chapter fourteen, the scene where Mary finds the overgrown key. Behind her, the hallway stretches farther than the blueprint allows, corridor accordioning into evening fields.

We look at each other a beat. Her pupils swallow nearly all iris. She says, not asks, “You ready?”

I taste pear alcohol at the back of my throat. Ready is a word I have lived beside but not entered. “I was in the middle of a conversation,” I say. “Through the wall. And the woman with numbers—”

“She’s already where she needs to be,” the child replies. “Numbers got tricky. She folded herself inside equation eight-thousand and three. It’s a spiral notebook in your kitchen drawer. You’ll open it someday during a power outage, pencil-only night, and the derivatives will still be warm.” She extends her free hand. The fingernails are chipped raspberry.

Against all logic, the act feels familiar, the kind you practice in dream drill until waking muscles memorize it. I lace my fingers through hers. Cool—not cold, not warm. Somewhere in between. At the junction of our palms, heat begins to pool, then migrate up my arm like mercury ascending glass.

We step out; the hallway peels away like wet wallpaper, revealing not elevator shafts but dunes of pale grass studded with rusted car parts. The moon is a quartz stone skipping across a black pond above us. It occurs to me I’m still in slippers—the absurd chicken-fluffy kind—but they sink slightly, comfortable as nests. The child leads us on a diagonal between pillars of abandoned fenders and crabapple trees holding small, hard fruit that gleams like red glass.

At some uncounted interval of steps, she speaks again. “Do you remember when we first met?”

I don’t, exactly. Not eggshell facts. Instead I feel the memory as texture—wet concrete under my bare knees circa age five, and a girl (her? me?) tracing spirals with a stub of chalk while explaining that any point along the curled line could technically be the beginning. My mother watched from a kitchen window, cigarette glowing, one hand twitching domestic semaphore: don’t move too far don’t disappear. The girl beside me erased the spiral with a socked foot and replaced it with an arrow pushing outward. “Exit is just entrance spelled backwards,” she’d said. Same voice.

Night air pushes the smell of gasoline and apple bruise through my hair. I try adult questions: “Where are we going? What are you part of?” But the child tightens her grip, swings our arms like skipping double-Dutch. She is timing a rhythm too intricate for mortal cadence.

A hill rises. Cresting it, we meet the Train. Everyone has such a train at least once, I think—the one that never appears on timetables or Google maps. Its cars are circa 1920s: burgundy lacquer, gold filigree like frost upon glass, windows lit with soft, buttery light. Steam unfurls in shapes of cut flowers. No engine as such, but a low humming beneath the frames, like a thousand captive bees.

The conductor is a figure I once dated, let’s call him E., the one who left mid-March citing “insufficient weather.” He stands at iron railing, beard trimmed academic, wearing a conductor’s cap tilted slightly too vain. His eyes hold the same half-smile that once broke me open like bread. He tips the cap. “Ticket?” he asks the child, not me.

She digs in the smock pocket of her T-shirt and withdraws a silver hoop earring, one I lost at an airport security line. The metal glints constellations. The conductor accepts it solemnly, presses it against the scan box which issues a lullaby minor key. He gestures us aboard. The step up is surprisingly high; I half stumble but the child catches. Strength unexpected in that tiny hinge of arms.

Inside, the car replicates my grandmother’s orchard kitchen—but elongated, Eschered: a farmer’s table bumpered with ladder-back chairs extends the full length of the carriage, bowls of real pears arranged every meter. Bees, not metaphorical, drift lazy loops above the fruit. Wallpaper is a pattern of floral snowshoes. The place smells of yeast gone wild, clove, and something feline. On one chair lounges a gray cat wearing a harness of postage stamps instead of fur. Each stamp shows a-picks from forgotten countries: Ubangi-Shari, Tanganyika, Ceylon by another name. The cat regards me, pupils square like old flashbulbs.

I sit across from my child self, who has now aged an imperceptible year; her T-shirt reads 2028. She plucks a pear from a bowl, twirls it until stem points north, sets it on its curve so it rocks slightly. “This is the one that holds your Question,” she informs me.

The pear is pale but around its equator runs a single red seam, thin as a paper cut. I touch it. The fruit feels warm, heart-echo warm. The car begins moving—not forward, not back, but in all cardinal directions simultaneously, the way spirographs draw flower-lattices; sums of vectors folding into spirals. The eggshell walls dissolve into a tunnel of orchard-night, black trunks pinwheeling.

“Instructions,” the child says. “Bite only when moon is exactly center.” I glance through the window; indeed, the quartz shard hangs dead ahead like a compass needle. The car aligns gradually, metal groans of celestial tectonics. While we wait, she begins:

“Once, there was a seamstress who could embroider silence. Not absence of sound—actual silence, the texture of it—onto cloth. People brought their loudest memories: mother’s pans clattering the night father left; the whistle of an empty hospital hallway; the click of the send button on an email they could never unsend. She stitched these into velvet squares and the world grew quieter stitch by stitch. One day a man arrived carrying only a polaroid of himself at age four, wearing snowsuit, smiling in a minus-thirty night so cold it made trees pop like rifle shots. He asked her to embroider the smile—not the sound, but its threat of vanishing. She agreed. When the last thread settled, he looked at the square and burst into tears, knowing he would never smile again without mourning the thing that had been preserved. It hurt too much. So he dissolved into the square itself, leaving only a second polaroid behind, this one blank. The seamstress kept both in a drawer.”

The child pauses, checks the moon. “Sound familiar?”

I swallow. Parts of it do. The story vibrates along bones titled mother, memory, sentence unfinished. Silence—actual silence—scratches at the threshold of hearing.

Moon now centre, white crown settling into the rearview. I lift the pear. It yields easily under teeth. First taste: Hungarian pear eau-de-vie, bittersweet combustion. Second taste: August and wet concrete. Third: hospital hallway, fluorescents humming at 3.17KHz (I measured once with a guitar pedal and restless insomnia). I chew slowly, letting flavours metamorphose, until the core arrives—a small metal object. I pull it free with tongue fingers. A circle. My lost silver earring, but looped now through a miniature iron key.

Across the table the child beams, age ticking another half-year; she is now eight, gap tooth winking. “Congratulations. You have what she always needed.”

Something jolts—the train brakes hard. Fruit bowls slide, pears sigh against wood, the stamp-cat yowls, letters flickering. Through the dusty glass of a door etched Exit in florid script, visible city leaks like spilled ink. Buildings turn themselves right-side-up; the night sky slots Paris-blue between roofs. I grip the key.

The child stands. “Time to disembark. Your stop is the third room in a house that doesn’t have rooms.” She walks me to the vestibule where E. the conductor waits. He holds out his palm, shows a small clock embedded in flesh. Second hand stutters around dragonflies. “Take care,” he says, voice the same smooth brick I once slept beside. The door folds open to nothing like origami reversing.

I step into mid-air—but the fall is courteous. I land softly on parquet I half-recognize: my grandmother’s kitchen floorboards, aged lighter except for the exact silhouette of a small body. In childhood I traced my length there daily while she baked, head at spice cupboard, feet near pantry drawer. The outline is still chalky from years of rubbing. I kneel, tracing the ghost-space, and see that the iron key fits into a knot-hole in the board—an eye I never before noticed. I insert the key. A quiet click. The room sighs sideways.

From the knot unfurls not a door, but a drawer wide as the floor itself. Inside is a miniature orchard at night. A single ladder-back chair waits; on it, the seamstress of the child’s story sits sewing something luminous. She looks up. My own face looks back, aged gracefully, silver streaks in black hair, eyes still erring on fear side of wonder. No seamstress, but me-reflected-forward. She gestures to the tin dish beside her filled with tiny Metropolis-silences: squares of black velvet, thread veins silver-white. Each square titled in stitching so fine only skin can read: argument (2011) apology letter unsent (2015) the way mother counted heartbeats against my sickbed pillow (1996) .

I open my mouth but she raises a hand—don’t speak, you’ll inflate the squares. She pats the beside her; I sit. The key warms in my palm, forgetting metal, opting bone. She takes it, threads it with crimson silk from a spool where color sings. Begins embroidering. I watch, mesmerized. The key becomes the stem of something wordless; the needle dances Morse through space between ache and articulation. Time is measured not in minutes but in the unclenching of my left shoulder, the way my breathing negotiates with pear alcohol.

Finally she knots the thread, bites with small perfect teeth. Hands me the square. All the silences hush. The room dims until only the velvet square glows. Words seep onto its surface: You were waiting to speak until you could say it right. Let wrongness sing.

I stare until the sentence rearranges letters, becomes a mouth. The square opens; the exhalation pushes into me like wind through smashed cathedral glass. Not words—more like raw weather, storm scented with forgiveness. I inhale until ribs tract like accordions. When the square goes dark, I find the seamstress-me has vanished; the drawer now holds ordinary darkness and the kettle whistle of my apartment building’s plumbing waking for the real 6:00 a.m.

I close the drawer—board slides back, keyhole fades. My phone vibrates on floor: 7:02 a.m., familiar world. No missed calls. The kitchen smells of pears again, but these are actual pears ripening in a bowl; their surfaces unmarred by red seams. Outside, city taxis bleat. Someone’s waffles burn. The falcon (Magnetic North never left, just diversified) is perched on the sill, eyeing the fruit. I crack the window.

As it eats, I notice one feather on its shoulder threaded with crimson silk. Tied to the end: a tiny rolled paper. I coax the bird, retrieve note.

Inside, in microscopic script: Exit is just entrance spelled backwards. Come back anytime; chickens-for-slippers not required. Also: take the basil to the rooftop at noon. It wants sun now.

I laugh then—startled how easy it is—sound startling into the day. For the first time in weeks, I check that the elevator hallway outside my apartment is merely itself: scuffed walls, one flyer for missing cat (found), unlit EXIT sign. The wall behind bedroom is quiet and flat. I make coffee, measure grounds ritualistic.

While I wait for water to boil, I open the “do not open until” dish. Inside: basil—resurrected, green tremor, roots in damp cotton. I lift it out gently; earth smell lofts. The janitor’s keys hum in lock downstairs. Somebody plays cello somewhere, low and repentant. I set the basil on windowsill to drink in honest light.

Later, I carry pot and spare soil to rooftop. City spreads underneath like a translation dictionary where every word is itself and something else simultaneously. I plant the basil in a deep box already warmed by stone. Bees come; they are the same three bees from train—recognizable by cinnamon klaxons on their wings— and they orbit basil politely, like monks adjusting robes. The air smells of gasoline and clove and something feline turned sun-struck herb. For the first second in memory, there is no unfinished sentence inside me; the parenthesis loop, having finally met itself, close into kiss.

From height, I see the child in yellow shirt eight years old walking hand in hand with an elderly woman who is clearly me, silver etching hair. They move along the sidewalk, entering a petrichor swirl. Neither looks up. For an instant I feel twin sensations: nostalgia forward, déjà vu backward. Then they are gone around corner, absorbed into the sleeve of moving city.

I stay on rooftop until dusk. The basil grows two centimetres (I watch). Its leaves develop a faint seam of red around edge, like fruit memories. Below, neon café signs flicker alive. A bus exhales brakes like the sigh of continents. My phone buzzes again. This time it’s a voicemail from unknown number. I play it on speaker. Silence at first, then: the seamstress voice—mine but wrapped years around—whispering thanks, the velvet square finally unfilled.

When I come back down, apartment greets me warm-fragrant; someone has baked white bread while I was gone. There is a note on counter in my own unknown handwriting: exited 3:17. Enter anytime.

I smile at the syntax, butter warm slice, open jars of pear jam left by ex-Buddhist-ex. The bread tastes like orchard rainfall. I eat slow, crumb by crumb, each bite casing a sphere of quiet-orchard-night around it, small but expanding. Night gathers outside; the building settles its shoulders like a tired orchestra.

And there, beneath fluorescent oven bulb, I begin the next beginning. First hum is soft, almost embarrassed. It is a story I begin to tell aloud, slowly, experimentally. It concerns a woman who woke at 3:17, yes, but now past tense; the hour no longer liquid because she has tasted its salt, carried its key, planted its seed. The story leaves the kitchen—floats past open window, rides dusk descending. Somewhere, the peregrine stirs, flexing stamped feather. Somewhere else, a seamstress sets down needle, knowing the fabric of night is not lack but loom.

When I finish I realize: 3:17 has resolved into 9:41 and everything is mercifully ordinary. I wash the plate. The faucet sings. Outside, sirens doppler east to west. Another emergency converging elsewhere. I wipe counter, close window. Lie down.

The dark comes gently, lid fitted, but no longer iron. Somewhere inside me now: a single unruptured breath shaped exactly like a spiral I can begin whenever ready.

The eggshell walls dissolve into a tunnel of orchard-night, black trunks pinwheeling.

“Instructions,” the child says. “Bite only when moon is exactly center.” I glance through the window; indeed, the quartz shard hangs dead ahead like a compass needle. The car aligns gradually, metal groans of celestial tectonics. While we wait, she begins:

“Once, there was a seamstress who could embroider silence. Not absence of sound—actual silence, the texture of it—onto cloth. People brought their loudest memories: mother’s pans clattering the night father left; the whistle of an empty hospital hallway; the click of the send button on an email they could never unsend. She stitched these into velvet squares and the world grew quieter stitch by stitch. One day a man arrived carrying only a polaroid of himself at age four, wearing snowsuit, smiling in a minus-thirty night so cold it made trees pop like rifle shots. He asked her to embroider the smile—not the sound, but its threat of vanishing. She agreed. When the last thread settled, he looked at the square and burst into tears, knowing he would never smile again without mourning the thing that had been preserved. It hurt too much. So he dissolved into the square itself, leaving only a second polaroid behind, this one blank. The seamstress kept both in a drawer.”

The child pauses, checks the moon. “Sound familiar?”

I swallow. Parts of it do. The story vibrates along bones titled mother, memory, sentence unfinished. Silence—actual silence—scratches at the threshold of hearing.

Moon now centre, white crown settling into the rearview. I lift the pear. It yields easily under teeth. First taste: Hungarian pear eau-de-vie, bittersweet combustion. Second taste: August and wet concrete. Third: hospital hallway, fluorescents humming at 3.17KHz (I measured once with a guitar pedal and restless insomnia). I chew slowly, letting flavours metamorphose, until the core arrives—a small metal object. I pull it free with tongue fingers. A circle. My lost silver earring, but looped now through a miniature iron key.

Across the table the child beams, age ticking another half-year; she is now eight, gap tooth winking. “Congratulations. You have what she always needed.”

Something jolts—the train brakes hard. Fruit bowls slide, pears sigh against wood, the stamp-cat yowls, letters flickering. Through the dusty glass of a door etched Exit in florid script, visible city leaks like spilled ink. Buildings turn themselves right-side-up; the night sky slots Paris-blue between roofs. I grip the key.

The child stands. “Time to disembark. Your stop is the third room in a house that doesn’t have rooms.” She walks me to the vestibule where E. the conductor waits. He holds out his palm, shows a small clock embedded in flesh. Second hand stutters around dragonflies. “Take care,” he says, voice the same smooth brick I once slept beside. The door folds open to nothing like origami reversing.

I step into mid-air—but the fall is courteous. I land softly on parquet I half-recognize: my grandmother’s kitchen floorboards, aged lighter except for the exact silhouette of a small body. In childhood I traced my length there daily while she baked, head at spice cupboard, feet near pantry drawer. The outline is still chalky from years of rubbing. I kneel, tracing the ghost-space, and see that the iron key fits into a knot-hole in the board—an eye I never before noticed. I insert the key. A quiet click. The room sighs sideways.

From the knot unfurls not a door, but a drawer wide as the floor itself. Inside is a miniature orchard at night. A single ladder-back chair waits; on it, the seamstress of the child’s story sits sewing something luminous. She looks up. My own face looks back, aged gracefully, silver streaks in black hair, eyes still erring on fear side of wonder. No seamstress, but me-reflected-forward. She gestures to the tin dish beside her filled with tiny Metropolis-silences: squares of black velvet, thread veins silver-white. Each square titled in stitching so fine only skin can read: argument (2011) apology letter unsent (2015) the way mother counted heartbeats against my sickbed pillow (1996) .

I open my mouth but she raises a hand—don’t speak, you’ll inflate the squares. She pats the beside her; I sit. The key warms in my palm, forgetting metal, opting bone. She takes it, threads it with crimson silk from a spool where color sings. Begins embroidering. I watch, mesmerized. The key becomes the stem of something wordless; the needle dances Morse through space between ache and articulation. Time is measured not in minutes but in the unclenching of my left shoulder, the way my breathing negotiates with pear alcohol.

Finally she knots the thread, bites with small perfect teeth. Hands me the square. All the silences hush. The room dims until only the velvet square glows. Words seep onto its surface: You were waiting to speak until you could say it right. Let wrongness sing.

I stare until the sentence rearranges letters, becomes a mouth. The square opens; the exhalation pushes into me like wind through smashed cathedral glass. Not words—more like raw weather, storm scented with forgiveness. I inhale until ribs tract like accordions. When the square goes dark, I find the seamstress-me has vanished; the drawer now holds ordinary darkness and the kettle whistle of my apartment building’s plumbing waking for the real 6:00 a.m.

I close the drawer—board slides back, keyhole fades. My phone vibrates on floor: 7:02 a.m., familiar world. No missed calls. The kitchen smells of pears again, but these are actual pears ripening in a bowl; their surfaces unmarred by red seams. Outside, city taxis bleat. Someone’s waffles burn. The falcon (Magnetic North never left, just diversified) is perched on the sill, eyeing the fruit. I crack the window.

As it eats, I notice one feather on its shoulder threaded with crimson silk. Tied to the end: a tiny rolled paper. I coax the bird, retrieve note.

Inside, in microscopic script: Exit is just entrance spelled backwards. Come back anytime; chickens-for-slippers not required. Also: take the basil to the rooftop at noon. It wants sun now.

I laugh then—startled how easy it is—sound startling into the day. For the first time in weeks, I check that the elevator hallway outside my apartment is merely itself: scuffed walls, one flyer for missing cat (found), unlit EXIT sign. The wall behind bedroom is quiet and flat. I make coffee, measure grounds ritualistic.

While I wait for water to boil, I open the “do not open until” dish. Inside: basil—resurrected, green tremor, roots in damp cotton. I lift it out gently; earth smell lofts. The janitor’s keys hum in lock downstairs. Somebody plays cello somewhere, low and repentant. I set the basil on windowsill to drink in honest light.

Later, I carry pot and spare soil to rooftop. City spreads underneath like a translation dictionary where every word is itself and something else simultaneously. I plant the basil in a deep box already warmed by stone. Bees come; they are the same three bees from train—recognizable by cinnamon klaxons on their wings— and they orbit basil politely, like monks adjusting robes. The air smells of gasoline and clove and something feline turned sun-struck herb. For the first second in memory, there is no unfinished sentence inside me; the parenthesis loop, having finally met itself, close into kiss.

From height, I see the child in yellow shirt eight years old walking hand in hand with an elderly woman who is clearly me, silver etching hair. They move along the sidewalk, entering a petrichor swirl. Neither looks up. For an instant I feel twin sensations: nostalgia forward, déjà vu backward. Then they are gone around corner, absorbed into the sleeve of moving city.

I stay on rooftop until dusk. The basil grows two centimetres (I watch). Its leaves develop a faint seam of red around edge, like fruit memories. Below, neon café signs flicker alive. A bus exhales brakes like the sigh of continents. My phone buzzes again. This time it’s a voicemail from unknown number. I play it on speaker. Silence at first, then: the seamstress voice—mine but wrapped years around—whispering thanks, the velvet square finally unfilled.

When I come back down, apartment greets me warm-fragrant; someone has baked white bread while I was gone. There is a note on counter in my own unknown handwriting: exited 3:17. Enter anytime.

I smile at the syntax, butter warm slice, open jars of pear jam left by ex-Buddhist-ex. The bread tastes like orchard rainfall. I eat slow, crumb by crumb, each bite casing a sphere of quiet-orchard-night around it, small but expanding. Night gathers outside; the building settles its shoulders like a tired orchestra.

And there, beneath fluorescent oven bulb, I begin the next beginning. First hum is soft, almost embarrassed. It is a story I begin to tell aloud, slowly, experimentally. It concerns a woman who woke at 3:17, yes, but now past tense; the hour no longer liquid because she has tasted its salt, carried its key, planted its seed. The story leaves the kitchen—floats past open window, rides dusk descending. Somewhere, the peregrine stirs, flexing stamped feather. Somewhere else, a seamstress sets down needle, knowing the fabric of night is not lack but loom.

When I finish I realize: 3:17 has resolved into 9:41 and everything is mercifully ordinary. I wash the plate. The faucet sings. Outside, sirens doppler east to west. Another emergency converging elsewhere. I wipe counter, close window. Lie down.

The dark comes gently, lid fitted, but no longer iron. Somewhere inside me now: a single unruptured breath shaped exactly like a spiral I can begin whenever ready.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
